From the actions of the Jews when they arrived, let us learn to begin with God, and to do what we can in worshiping God when we cannot do what we want. They could not immediately have a temple, but they would not be without an altar. Fear of danger should prompt us to our duty. Do we have many enemies? Then it is good to have God as our Friend, and to maintain fellowship with him. Our fears should drive us to prayer. The sacrifices for all these observances were a heavy expense for such a poor group; yet besides those specifically appointed, many brought voluntary offerings to the Lord. And they made preparations for building the temple without delay: whatever God calls us to do, we may depend on his care to provide us with what is needed.

There was a striking combination of feelings when laying the foundation of the temple. Those who only knew the misery of having no temple at all praised the Lord with shouts of joy. To them, even this foundation seemed great. We ought to be thankful for the beginnings of mercy, though it is not yet complete. But those who remembered the glory of the first temple, and considered how much less impressive this one would likely be, wept aloud. There was reason for it, and if they mourned the sin that caused this sad change, they did well. Yet it was wrong to dampen the common joy. They scorned the day of small beginnings, and were unthankful for the good they enjoyed. Let not the remembrance of past troubles overwhelm the awareness of present blessings.
